title: Why strangers fall silent in lifts
summary: Explain why a lift turns talkative people mute, and what that thirty-second truce reveals about how cities teach us to share small spaces with strangers.
topic: The unwritten rules that govern how strangers behave in a lift
prompt: Write a 450–600-word explanatory essay on why people who chat freely on the street fall silent the moment a lift door closes, and what this brief, awkward truce reveals about how cities train us to share tight spaces. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and complex concession (although / even though).
hintStarters: What looks like rudeness in a lift is really a kind of courtesy that…
Although the space is shared, the silence is not so much chosen as…
It is the absence of an exit, more than the presence of strangers, that…
The moment the doors close, an unspoken agreement is reached about where to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: một quy tắc bất thành văn
giữ ý, không can dự vào người khác
một sự đình chiến / hưu chiến
chia sẻ một không gian chung
tỏ ra nhận biết sự có mặt của ai
một khế ước bất thành văn

sampleText:
Watch the same two people on a pavement and then in a lift, and you will see two different animals. On the street they laugh, gesture, and let their voices carry; the doors close, and within a second they have gone quiet, eyes lifted to the numbers above the door as though the floor count were the most absorbing reading of their lives. Nothing has happened. No one has been threatened or offended. Yet a rule has come into force, and everyone obeys it without ever having been taught.

What looks like rudeness in a lift is really a kind of courtesy that has nowhere else to go. On the street, distance does the work of politeness for us: if a stranger stands too close, we simply step aside. In a lift there is no aside. Four people are pressed into a box the size of a wardrobe, and the one thing that usually keeps strangers comfortable — the freedom to move away — has been taken from them. So another tool is reached for instead. We cannot create physical distance, so we manufacture a kind of mental distance, looking past one another, lowering our voices, pretending, politely, that the others are not quite there. The sociologist Erving Goffman gave this its name: civil inattention, the small ritual by which we signal that we have noticed someone and mean them no harm, precisely by declining to stare.

It is the absence of an exit, more than the presence of strangers, that produces the hush. Consider how the same people behave on a crowded but moving train, where there is at least the fiction of somewhere to go; conversation survives there in a way it rarely does between floors. Or consider what happens when the lift breaks down. The first thirty seconds are unbearable, and then, abruptly, people begin to talk — because the truce was never about disliking one another. It was about not knowing where to put oneself, and a stalled lift finally gives everyone a subject.

Although the space is shared, the silence is not so much chosen as imposed by the architecture itself. The box decides our behaviour before we do. This is why the rules barely change from Lagos to Helsinki: the room is the same shape everywhere, and a shape can be a stricter teacher than any culture. Children learn it without instruction. A four-year-old who shouts happily in a supermarket will fall silent in a lift, having absorbed, from nothing but watching, that this small room asks for a quieter self.

Some will object that the silence is simply British reserve, or urban coldness, dressed up as theory — that warmer, more sociable places fill their lifts with chatter. There is something to this; the volume does vary, and a crowded lift in Naples is not a crowded lift in Tokyo. Even so, the underlying move is the same everywhere. Where talk continues, eye contact still drops; where greetings are exchanged, they are kept short and aimed at no one in particular. The friendliness is real, but it is rationed, and it is rationed by the same pressure of enclosure.

The lift, then, is a tiny laboratory of city life. In thirty seconds it asks the question every crowded place asks more slowly: how do you stay decent to people you cannot get away from? The answer the lift teaches — look away, speak softly, leave the others their dignity — is the quiet grammar on which the whole crowded city quietly runs.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-cleft to isolate the real cause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is the absence of an exit, more than the presence of strangers, that produces the hush' dùng cấu trúc chẻ câu it-cleft để tách ra nguyên nhân thật sự (việc thiếu lối ra) khỏi cái nguyên nhân ta hay ngỡ là đúng (sự có mặt của người lạ).
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the absence of an exit, more than the presence of strangers, that produces the hush' uses an it-cleft to isolate the true cause (no exit) from the assumed one (the strangers themselves).

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession + passive to show the silence is imposed, not chosen
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although the space is shared, the silence is not so much chosen as imposed by the architecture itself' ghép mệnh đề nhượng bộ với 'although' và dạng bị động 'is … imposed by' để cho thấy hành vi đến từ căn phòng chứ không từ ý muốn con người.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although the space is shared, the silence is not so much chosen as imposed by the architecture itself' pairs an 'although' concession clause with the passive 'is … imposed by' to show the behaviour comes from the room, not from human choice.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hãy quan sát cùng hai người ấy trên vỉa hè rồi trong thang máy, và bạn sẽ thấy hai sinh vật khác hẳn nhau. Trên phố họ cười nói, khua tay, để giọng vang xa; cửa đóng lại, và chỉ trong một giây họ đã im bặt, mắt ngước lên những con số phía trên cửa như thể con số tầng là thứ đọc cuốn hút nhất đời họ. Chẳng có gì xảy ra. Không ai bị đe dọa hay xúc phạm. Vậy mà một quy tắc đã có hiệu lực, và ai cũng tuân theo dù chưa từng được dạy.

Cái trông như sự khiếm nhã trong thang máy thực ra là một dạng phép lịch sự không còn chỗ nào để đi. Trên phố, khoảng cách làm thay ta cái việc của phép lịch sự: nếu một người lạ đứng quá gần, ta chỉ việc bước sang một bên. Trong thang máy chẳng có chỗ để bước sang. Bốn người bị ép vào một cái hộp cỡ một chiếc tủ quần áo, và cái thứ thường giúp người lạ thấy thoải mái — quyền tự do bước ra xa — đã bị tước khỏi họ. Thế nên ta với lấy một công cụ khác. Không thể tạo ra khoảng cách vật lý, ta bèn chế tạo ra một dạng khoảng cách trong tâm trí, nhìn lướt qua nhau, hạ giọng, giả vờ, một cách lịch sự, rằng những người kia gần như không có ở đó. Nhà xã hội học Erving Goffman đã đặt tên cho điều này: sự thờ ơ lịch sự, nghi thức nhỏ mà qua đó ta báo hiệu rằng ta đã nhận ra ai đó và không có ý hại họ, chính bằng cách từ chối nhìn chằm chằm.

Chính sự thiếu vắng một lối ra, hơn là sự hiện diện của người lạ, mới tạo ra sự im ắng. Hãy xét xem cùng những người ấy cư xử ra sao trên một chuyến tàu đông nhưng đang chạy, nơi ít nhất vẫn còn cái ảo tưởng rằng có chỗ nào đó để đi; ở đó câu chuyện sống sót theo cách mà nó hiếm khi làm được giữa các tầng lầu. Hoặc hãy xét điều gì xảy ra khi thang máy hỏng. Ba mươi giây đầu thật không chịu nổi, rồi đột nhiên, người ta bắt đầu nói chuyện — bởi sự hưu chiến ấy chưa bao giờ là vì người ta ghét nhau. Nó là về việc không biết đặt mình vào đâu, và một chiếc thang máy chết máy cuối cùng cho mọi người một chủ đề.

Dù không gian là chung, sự im lặng không hẳn là được chọn mà là bị chính kiến trúc áp đặt. Cái hộp quyết định hành vi của ta trước cả khi ta quyết. Đó là lý do các quy tắc gần như không đổi từ Lagos đến Helsinki: căn phòng có hình dáng giống nhau ở khắp nơi, và một hình khối có thể là người thầy nghiêm khắc hơn bất kỳ nền văn hóa nào. Trẻ con học điều đó mà không cần ai dạy. Một đứa bé bốn tuổi reo hò vui vẻ trong siêu thị sẽ im bặt trong thang máy, đã hấp thụ, từ chẳng gì khác ngoài việc quan sát, rằng căn phòng nhỏ này đòi hỏi một phiên bản im lặng hơn của mình.

Một số người sẽ phản đối rằng sự im lặng chỉ là sự dè dặt kiểu Anh, hay sự lạnh lùng đô thị, được khoác áo lý thuyết — rằng những nơi ấm áp, hòa đồng hơn lấp đầy thang máy của họ bằng chuyện trò. Điều này cũng có lý; âm lượng quả có thay đổi, và một thang máy đông đúc ở Naples không giống một thang máy đông đúc ở Tokyo. Dẫu vậy, động thái cơ bản ở đâu cũng như nhau. Nơi câu chuyện vẫn tiếp diễn, giao tiếp bằng mắt vẫn rơi xuống; nơi lời chào được trao đi, chúng được giữ ngắn gọn và không nhắm vào ai cụ thể. Sự thân thiện là có thật, nhưng nó được phân phối nhỏ giọt, và nó bị hạn chế bởi cùng một áp lực của sự khép kín.

Vậy thì thang máy là một phòng thí nghiệm tí hon của đời sống thành phố. Trong ba mươi giây nó đặt ra câu hỏi mà mọi nơi đông đúc đều đặt ra một cách chậm rãi hơn: làm sao để vẫn tử tế với những người mà bạn không thể tránh đi đâu được? Câu trả lời mà thang máy dạy cho ta — nhìn đi chỗ khác, nói nhỏ, để lại cho người khác phẩm giá của họ — chính là thứ ngữ pháp thầm lặng mà cả thành phố đông đúc lặng lẽ vận hành dựa trên đó.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to single out the true cause of the silence?
options: The absence of an exit produces the hush, which is more than strangers there.
It is the absence of an exit, more than the presence of strangers, that produces the hush.
Producing the hush is what the absence of an exit the strangers does.
The hush is produced an exit absence more than strangers it is that.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X … that …' is an it-cleft that foregrounds the lack of an exit as the real cause.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is X … that …' frame.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence opens with a concession clause and continues naturally?
options: Although the space is shared, but the silence is imposed by the architecture also.
The space is shared, although imposed the silence by architecture not chosen.
Although the space is shared, the silence is not so much chosen as imposed by the architecture itself.
Although shared the space, the silence chosen is not so imposed architecture itself.
mcQuestions.explanation: The 'Although …, …' clause concedes the sharing and then states the main point without a redundant 'but'.
mcQuestions.hint: A clause starting with 'although' should not be followed by 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to show the freedom is removed by the space, not by a person?
options: The space took away their freedom to move which is comfortable.
Taking the freedom away is what them move comfortable does.
Move away freedom from them the space their taken has comfortable.
the freedom to move away — has been taken from them
mcQuestions.explanation: 'has been taken from them' is a passive that hides the agent, suggesting the loss is structural.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'has been + past participle' without naming who acts?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'civil inattention' mean as the essay uses it?
options: Politely signalling you have noticed someone and mean no harm, by not staring
Refusing to obey city authorities
Ignoring a problem until it becomes serious
Being rude to strangers on purpose
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay defines it as the ritual of acknowledging someone precisely by declining to stare.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the sentence that names Goffman.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces the counter-argument most fairly?
options: Some people are just cold and antisocial and that explains it.
Some will object that the silence is simply British reserve, or urban coldness, dressed up as theory.
Obviously only unfriendly cultures have quiet lifts at all.
Anyone who stays silent in a lift clearly hates other people.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Some will object that …' fairly states the opposing cultural explanation before the essay answers it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option names a real objection instead of insulting people?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option hedges the cultural variation appropriately?
options: All warm cultures completely fill their lifts with endless conversation.
Culture never has any effect on lift behaviour whatsoever.
There is something to this; the volume does vary, and a crowded lift in Naples is not a crowded lift in Tokyo.
Naples and Tokyo lifts the same conversation always have no difference.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'There is something to this' concedes partial truth and 'does vary' hedges the claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that admits the point partly holds.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why do people start talking when a lift breaks down?
options: Because they suddenly start to like each other
Because silence is illegal during an emergency
Because they were never really silent before
Because the breakdown finally gives everyone a shared subject
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says the stalled lift 'finally gives everyone a subject', ending the awkwardness.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent explanatory register?
options: On the street, distance does the work of politeness for us.
Honestly lifts are super awkward and everyone hates them, right?
Basically people just zone out because lifts are boring, simple as that.
It's kind of weird how nobody talks, like, ever, in those things.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B is precise and analytical; the others slip into casual speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the train example back to the main claim?
options: Anyway, trains are completely different and unrelated to this.
Or consider what happens when the lift breaks down.
In conclusion, lifts and trains are exactly the same thing.
On the contrary, no one ever talks on any train ever.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Or consider …' adds a second supporting case while staying on the same line of argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase introduces a further example smoothly?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to keep to oneself' mean here?
options: To talk loudly to everyone present
To hold one's breath
To stay quiet and avoid intruding on others
To leave the building
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Keep to oneself' means staying private and not engaging the people around you.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of someone deliberately not bothering others.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: looking past one another, to lower our voices, and we pretend the others away
look past one another, the voices lowering, and pretending not the others there
to look past, lowering voices our, and pretend the others away there
looking past one another, lowering our voices, pretending the others are not quite there
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched '-ing' phrases form a clean parallel list.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three verbs in the same '-ing' form?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central point about the lift?
options: That the lift compresses, into thirty seconds, the question of how to stay decent to people you cannot avoid
That people in lifts secretly dislike one another
That lifts should be redesigned to encourage conversation
That silence in lifts proves cities make people lonely
mcQuestions.explanation: The final paragraph frames the lift as a tiny laboratory of the city's central social question.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'a truce' mean in this context?
options: A formal legal agreement
A temporary, unspoken agreement to leave each other in peace
A loud argument between strangers
A type of lift mechanism
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay treats the silence as a quiet, temporary peace between strangers.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a brief, informal ceasefire.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best captures the essay's nuanced conclusion?
options: Silence in lifts is simply rudeness that cities should fix.
People in lifts are colder than people anywhere else.
The lift teaches a small grammar of decency — look away, speak softly, leave others their dignity — that the whole crowded city runs on.
Lifts prove that strangers can never be polite to one another.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion reframes the silence as a learned courtesy underpinning crowded urban life.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that treats the silence as courtesy, not coldness.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an em-dash insertion correctly to define a term mid-sentence?
options: civil inattention by which the ritual we signal — noticed someone — mean no harm declining stare.
the small ritual — civil inattention noticed — by which we signal mean no harm them.
we signal civil inattention — the ritual that — noticed declining no harm them stare.
civil inattention, the small ritual by which we signal that we have noticed someone and mean them no harm, precisely by declining to stare
mcQuestions.explanation: The appositive after 'civil inattention' defines it cleanly and reads as a full sentence.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets the definition follow the term in order?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'proximity' mean as used in the vocabulary of this topic?
options: Nearness in physical space
The act of approving of someone
A polite greeting
A type of exit
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Proximity' means closeness or nearness, the condition a lift forces on strangers.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of how physically near the strangers are.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of the last time you fell silent among strangers in a tight space. Were you being cold, or were you, in fact, being considerate in the only way the room allowed — and what would happen to a crowded city if everyone stopped observing that quiet rule?
